Concept Memo · Nordea Head of AWM AI · Job ID 4462 · 2026-06-01

A working slice of the AWM advisor flow

Follow-up to my May 22 application. Rather than a courtesy nudge, this is a working artifact: a five-stage agentic system that drafts a private-banking Monday brief for a fictitious Nordic family office, paired with the strategy, governance, eval, and 90-day thinking behind it. Built in a week on Vertex AI Gemini 2.5. The live demo is one click away; this memo is the reasoning.

From · Alim Polat
To · Thomas Vedel Kristensen · Nordea AWM
Live demo · alimpolat-nordea-awm-poc.hf.space
Date · Mon Jun 1, 2026
01 · Why this exists

Instead of a soft nudge, a working artifact

Note to Thomas Follow-up · Job ID 4462

Dear Thomas,

I applied for the Head of AWM AI role on May 22. The standard senior-application follow-up is a polite email asking whether you've had a chance to review. I'd rather show you what I'd actually do in the seat.

So over the last week I built a working slice of the mandate as you wrote it — "enabling the private banking advisor flow end-to-end, from opportunity identification and client insights to meeting preparation, recommendations, and follow-up." It runs live, you can chat with it, and this memo explains the architecture, the eval, the governance posture, and how I'd spend the first 90 days. I've been deliberate about what it does not do, too — an oversold POC is worse than none.

The point isn't to prove that retrieval-augmented generation works in 2026; you already know it does. The point is that I've thought specifically about your advisor-flow problem and shipped a credible first cut of it — which is, I hope, a more useful signal than a CV bullet.

Best regards,
Alim Polat

02 · Reading the mandate

The five-stage advisor flow, mapped to features

The load-bearing phrase in the job description is the five-stage advisor flow. I took it literally and built something that visibly touches every stage — then was honest about which stages are deep and which are deliberately shallow at POC scope.

JD stageWhat it means concretelyPOC implementation
1 · Opportunity identificationDetect signals in the portfolio + market that flag a conversation worth having"Signals & Opportunities" panel — the system surfaces portfolio-drift and IPS-breach triggers (e.g. Gulf real estate 5pp over target; SEK floor breached) deep
2 · Client insightsSynthesize what we know about the client into a usable picture"Client overview" — holdings, cost basis, FX exposure, stated concerns, restrictions, last-meeting context shallow
3 · Meeting preparationThe advisor's working document for the meetingThe "Monday brief" itself — macro context + portfolio context + weekend intelligence, all cited deep
4 · RecommendationsConcrete next-best-actions the advisor can proposeThree NBAs, each with rationale, evidence references, projected impact, computation trace, and HITL controls deep
5 · Follow-upCapture meeting outcomes and propagate forward"After-meeting capture" panel — a mocked note field with an explicit "this would feed the next brief" loop shallow
Honest depth-of-demo. Stages 1, 3, and 4 get the deepest implementation — that's where the advisor value concentrates and where the agentic and retrieval work is genuinely interesting. Stages 2 and 5 are present and visible but intentionally thin. I'd rather name that plainly than have you discover it. Naming the scope honestly is the same discipline I'd bring to reporting a pilot's KPIs to leadership.
03 · The demo

What you'll see when you open the URL

The live demo is at alimpolat-nordea-awm-poc.hf.space — no login, no tutorial. It opens on a pre-rendered Monday brief for the Bergström Family Office, a fictitious Stockholm-based UHNW client (SEK 480M, global tilt, third-generation industrial wealth). The brief is generated by the agentic pipeline and cached, so the page renders instantly rather than making you wait through a spinner. Below are four captures of the interface.

01 · The Monday brief
Client overview, target-vs-actual allocation, and the holdings table with seven single-name IPS breaches flagged in rust.
02 · A next-best-action with HITL controls
Each NBA carries a confidence label, a rationale, a projected-impact chart, a computation trace, and Approve / Edit / Regenerate / Reject.
03 · The citation drawer
Clicking "evidence" opens the underlying source chunks — the IPS, the portfolio statement, the grounded news item — so every claim is traceable.
04 · Chat to interrogate
The advisor can ask follow-up questions mid-review; answers are cited and return in <5s on Gemini 2.5 Flash.

Three prompts worth trying

The chat panel is pre-seeded with suggested prompts so the "what do I try" is obvious. These three exercise the different specialists behind the system:

“What if Brent drops to $60?”
“Any Gulf real estate news?”
“What's our ECB-rate exposure?”

What the HITL controls do

Every recommendation in the brief carries four controls. They are the visible answer to the JD's "robust human-in-the-loop controls" requirement — the advisor, not the model, stays accountable.

✓ Approve ✎ Edit ↻ Regenerate ✕ Reject
  • Approve — marks the action advisor-endorsed; in production this becomes a positive training signal.
  • Edit — inline override of the recommendation text before it's used.
  • Regenerate — re-runs the action with an optional natural-language nudge ("focus on the FX hedge first").
  • Reject — logged with a reason; the rejection itself is signal for the next cycle.
One note on hosting, surfaced up front. The demo is deployed on Hugging Face Spaces — free, no card, public URL — rather than Cloud Run, because the GCP sandbox I was given is scoped to Vertex AI only (no deploy permissions). The architecture is portable: the same container runs on Cloud Run unchanged, and the Vertex AI calls in this demo already run on the provisioned GCP project via the service account. The production target is an EU-region Cloud Run behind VPC-SC and CMEK (see §5). I'd rather show you a working thing on free infrastructure than a slide deck about a perfect one.
04 · Architecture

A five-stage agentic flow, Gemini-native

The system operationalizes the advisor flow as five named stage agents, sequenced by a thin async orchestrator. Stages 1 and 2 (Opportunity-Scout, Client-Insights) prepare the ground; a Planner (Gemini 2.5 Pro) then fans out to four specialists that run in parallel; a Synthesizer (Gemini 2.5 Pro) composes the final schema-enforced brief; Stage 5 (Follow-up) closes the loop back to Stage 1.

Five-stage agentic flow · request top-down
Opportunity-Scout + Client-Insights → Planner fans out to 4 parallel specialists → Synthesizer → brief → Follow-up loops back
Brief generation ~15–18s (parallel-specialist bound) · pre-generated and cached · chat <5s

The model layer

  • Gemini 2.5 Pro — Planner and Synthesizer (the reasoning-heavy steps that see the whole problem once).
  • Gemini 2.5 Flash — the four specialists, the listwise reranker, query rewriting, and chat (parallel, fast, cheap).
  • gemini-embedding-001 @ 768d — task-typed embeddings (RETRIEVAL_DOCUMENT at index, RETRIEVAL_QUERY at query time), an easy quality lever that 2024-era pipelines tend to leave on the floor.

The retrieval pipeline

Recommendations are only as good as what they're grounded in, so the retrieval stack is the part I spent the most care on. It is a hybrid pipeline drawn from the current state of the art (see the Atlas RAG appendix, §10):

  • Hybrid retrieval — dense vectors over an embedded Qdrant collection plus sparse BM25 (rank_bm25, in-process), each top-30.
  • Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF, k=60) merges the two lists, then a Gemini Flash listwise reranker orders the survivors down to a top-5.
  • Anthropic Contextual Retrieval — at index time each chunk is prepended with an LLM-generated context line ("this chunk is from the Bergström IPS, FX-policy section…"), the technique Anthropic reports cuts retrieval failures by roughly half.
  • Single container — FastAPI backend, React + ECharts frontend, Qdrant embedded in-process. No external database to fail, no inter-service latency, deterministic and demo-safe.

Two live views from the brief

The interface is visually rich and themed throughout with Apache ECharts. Two examples, rendered here from the same Bergström snapshot data the demo uses:

Current Allocation
Market-value weight by asset class · SEK 480M AUM
Computed from holdings fixture · not generated
Macro Signal Radar
Portfolio risk dimensions · weekend snapshot vs benchmark
FX risk and IPS-compliance are the two red dimensions
Why this shape. A single container with an embedded vector store is the simplest thing that demonstrates a genuinely agentic, well-grounded pipeline without a rack of external dependencies. It reads as 2026 — task-typed embeddings, contextual chunking, hybrid + RRF + listwise rerank, schema-enforced agent outputs — while staying within an envelope I could deploy and you could open in one click. The reasoning behind each choice is in the Atlas RAG appendix (§10).
05 · Eval & governance

The trust receipt

At a Tier-1 bank the demo is the easy part; the eval is what survives a model-risk review. I ran a retrieval and faithfulness eval over a synthetic Q&A set built from the corpus, judged by Gemini 2.5 Flash. The headline numbers:

0.91
Retrieval
recall@5
0.96
Faithfulness
(grounded in source)
1.0
Answer
relevance
✓
Unanswerable Q →
"I don't know"

The fourth tile matters as much as the first three: on a question the corpus cannot answer, the system correctly declined rather than fabricating. "I don't know" is a valid — and, in this domain, a required — output. Every call is traced in Phoenix for observability, so a reviewer can open any brief and walk from the rendered claim back through the synthesizer, the specialists, and the exact retrieved chunks.

Where I'm being honest about the eval

Two caveats, stated plainly. First, the scores above come from a custom Gemini-Flash judge I wrote, not the off-the-shelf Ragas harness — the OSS Ragas + Gemini path hit a version quirk during the build, so I bypassed it rather than ship numbers I couldn't reproduce. That means I'm not reporting Ragas context-precision / context-recall here; I'm reporting what my own judge measured. Second, the clear weak spot is four-document synthesis — questions that require stitching evidence across four sources score around recall 0.65, well below the single-/two-hop cases. That's the first thing I'd harden, and I'd name it to leadership before they found it.

The HITL stance that survives a model-risk review

  • Every NBA cites its evidence. No recommendation rests on an uncited claim; the citation drawer exposes the underlying chunks.
  • Confidence labels are mandatory. Every item carries high / medium / low-needs-verification. No silent confidence.
  • "I don't know" is a first-class output. When retrieval returns nothing usable, the agent says so.
  • Quantities are computed, not generated. Portfolio math runs through Gemini code execution and carries a computation trace; the FX-floor breach is arithmetic, not a guess.
  • Outputs are schema-enforced. Every brief deserializes cleanly into a Pydantic BriefSchema; a malformed output fails validation and triggers regenerate, never silent garbage.
  • The advisor remains accountable. The AI is a fast, cited, schema-enforced first draft; the human approves, edits, or rejects each item.

This is the same posture I built into SmartDraft at ICON — regulated clinical-trial documents where a hallucinated citation is a sanctionable-grade error, so every output was tier-gated, audit-trailed, and grounded. The advisor flow is the same problem with different nouns.

06 · KPI mapping

Every capability mapped to a Nordea KPI

The JD names four KPI categories: advisor-capacity uplift, faster time-to-advice, improved client conversion/retention, and reduced operational risk and cost. Each capability in the POC maps to one explicitly — because "we built a cool demo" is not a business case, and the first thing I'd be asked to do in the seat is report quantified impact to leadership.

POC capabilityNordea KPI categoryHow it moves the number
Auto-generated Monday briefAdvisor capacity uplift30–45 min of pre-meeting research compressed to a 3–5 min review
Chat-to-interrogateTime-to-adviceMid-meeting questions answered in <5s instead of "let me get back to you"
Citation-grounded recommendationsRisk & cost reductionEvery NBA traceable to a source doc — audit-ready, fewer compliance review cycles
Schema-enforced agent outputsRisk & cost reductionNo free-form hallucination; structured payload feeds a downstream CRM cleanly
Code-executed portfolio mathRisk & cost reductionQuantities computed not generated — removes a whole category of LLM error
Opportunity-spotting from driftClient conversion / retentionProactive trigger for advisor outreach — catches under-engaged accounts
Approve / Edit / Regenerate / Reject HITLRisk & cost reductionAdvisor stays accountable; AI augments rather than decides — regulator-defensible
07 · 90-day plan

How I'd spend the first 90 days as Head of AWM AI

A POC shows I can build. This shows how I'd lead. The throughline is eval-first delivery and disciplined scope: diagnose before changing anything, ship one real thing, then earn the roadmap with a KPI report.

Days 1–30Diagnose — don't change anything yet
  • Meet the AI & Advanced Analytics team I'd inherit or peer with; understand its size, composition, and morale before touching it.
  • Build a stakeholder map across Private Banking leadership, the COO function, Technology, and Control — the four partners the JD names.
  • Inventory all in-flight AI work and classify each item: pause, continue, or accelerate. Kill nothing in month one without evidence.
  • Understand what "production" means at Nordea today, and what vendors/partners are already engaged so I don't propose what's already in motion.
Days 31–60Ship one real thing — eval-first
  • Pick one production-grade use case from the five-stage flow. Meeting prep is the natural first — highest advisor-capacity payoff, most contained scope, exactly the slice this POC demonstrates.
  • Recruit two private-banking advisors as design partners from day one; the eval rubric is co-authored with the people who'll use it.
  • Run a four-week, eval-first build: define the brief-quality rubric and the pass bar before writing the agent, then build to it.
  • Ship to a limited pilot — a handful of advisors, real (EU-resident) data behind the production governance perimeter.
Days 61–90Prove it, then earn the roadmap
  • Run a pilot retrospective with the two design-partner advisors; capture what worked, what didn't, and what to harden.
  • Deliver a KPI report to senior leadership against the four JD categories — quantified, honest about limits.
  • Propose a Q3–Q4 roadmap covering two to three additional use cases, sequenced by value and feasibility.
  • Put forward a hiring plan where the team has gaps (see §9).
08 · What this does not do

Honest scope

You'll catch an oversold POC in thirty seconds, so let me save you the trouble. This is a one-week artifact with deliberate limits:

  • Single persona. One client (Bergström), one advisor. No multi-tenant model, no user management.
  • Synthetic data. Every holding, figure, and meeting note is invented. No real Nordea client data touches the system — which is also why the GDPR posture in §5 is clean.
  • No auth. The unguessable URL is the only credential; the page carries a noindex tag. Fine for a demo, not for production.
  • Snapshot-first intel. Structured World_Monitor market signals run from a curated snapshot because the public endpoints are API-gated — but the weekend news is live (see below).
  • Custom-judge eval. The numbers come from my own Gemini judge, not the standard Ragas harness (§5).
  • No production audit trail beyond default logging, no persistent DB, no CI/CD beyond a single deploy command, no load testing, English only.
Two things that are genuinely live, not faked. The weekend news in the brief is real: the News specialist uses Google Search grounding, so the headlines (the UAE realty slowdown, the Saudi Q1 property data, the EU Green Bond Standard) are real, current, and carry real citation URIs — the principle is "live where reachable, deterministic snapshot fallback otherwise." And the Vertex AI calls run on the provisioned GCP project, not a personal key. The synthetic parts are synthetic on purpose; the parts that demonstrate live grounding are actually live.
Prompt-injection, handled. Grounded web content is untrusted by construction. The News specialist returns only a schema-enforced {headline, source_uri, tag} object — never raw web text — and the Synthesizer consumes structured specialist outputs, not raw grounded prose. An instruction injected into a web page therefore cannot reach a system prompt; agents treat retrieved content as data, never as instructions, and a malformed payload fails Pydantic validation rather than being silently obeyed.
09 · First three hires

If the team has gaps, these come first

Roles, not names — and only if the diagnosis in days 1–30 shows the gaps are real:

  • ML engineer, RAG-in-production. Someone who has run retrieval and agentic pipelines under real load and latency budgets — not a notebook prototyper. This is the person who turns the POC pattern into something that survives a thousand advisors.
  • Advisor-domain product manager. Someone fluent in the private-banking workflow who keeps the build honest about what advisors actually need, owns the design-partner relationship, and translates front-office needs into use cases.
  • MLOps / eval engineer. Someone who owns the eval harness, observability, model-risk evidence, and deployment hygiene — the function that makes "regulator-defensible" a property of the system, not a slide.
10 · Appendix

Staying current with emerging AI

The JD asks the Head of AWM AI to "stay current with emerging AI technologies relevant to asset and wealth management." The architecture choices in §4 aren't arbitrary — each one traces to a documented review of the current RAG state of the art that I wrote during this build (the "Atlas" research note). It covers why task-typed embeddings, contextual chunking, hybrid + RRF + listwise reranking, and a bounded multi-agent pattern beat the 2023-era defaults, and why I deliberately avoided a knowledge graph and the heavier orchestration frameworks for a project this size.

The full source is browsable on the demo's Hugging Face Space (the Files tab), and the README, ARCHITECTURE, and EVAL write-ups plus the Atlas research note (docs/research/atlas-rag-sota-v2_2026-05-24.html) are in the project repository, which I'm glad to share — on a call or via a read-only link.

The ask. If this is the kind of thinking and execution you want leading AWM AI, I'd welcome a screening conversation — the team's current AI portfolio, the advisor segments you're targeting first, and how I'd approach the build for real. The demo is at alimpolat-nordea-awm-poc.hf.space; I'm Stockholm-based and flexible on timing.
